data.title: BRP Gains Market Share as New Models Lift Sales and Outlook — Update
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

BRP is gaining ground on rivals across its core product lineup, with the Canadian recreational vehicle maker reporting higher sales of off-road vehicles, snowmobiles and ATVs in the fourth quarter.

The Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo makers North American retail sales rose 12% in the quarter, lifted by stronger volume after late season snowfalls had weighed on results a year earlier. BRP attributed the share gains to a wave of new model launches across its portfolio that have resonated with buyers and widened its position in each category.

"We significantly outpaced the recreational-vehicle industry in the fourth quarter, a trend that began in October when our new models were reaching dealers showroom," Chief Executive Denis Le Vot said on a call with investors Thursday.

Shares were up 4.2% to 93.28 Canadian dollars ($67.53) in late morning trading. The stock is up 90% in the last 52 weeks.

The leisure‑vehicle industry has struggled in the past two years as post-pandemic demand normalized, interest rates climbed, and dealers worked through excess inventory, pressuring orders across powersports, marine, and recreational vehicles as a whole.

The company said the momentum that started in the prior quarter is continuing into the new year. For fiscal 2027, BRP is targeting revenue growth of C$8.9 billion and C$9.15 billion, up from C$8.44 billion in fiscal 2026, and ahead of market expectations. The growth is expected to come from all its main segments, with strong gains in year-round products, its largest unit.

Net income is expected to rise to C$410 million to C$480 million, from C$340.4 million in the previous fiscal year. Normalized earnings--an adjusted figure--are expected at C$5.50 a share to C$6.50 a share, with analyst forecasts of C$6.11.

In its fourth-quarter ended Jan. 31, the company logged a profit of C$45.8 million, or C$0.63 a share, compared with a loss of C$50.5 million, or C$0.68 a share, in the comparable quarter a year earlier.

Revenue rose 16% to C$2.46 billion, topping forecasts thanks to higher sales of off-road vehicles and personal watercraft.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.description:
Revenue from continuing operations grew 5.5% year-over-year to HK$237.5 million, with profit from continuing operations at HK$6.8 million. The Group completed the disposal of its property development business, improving its gearing ratio and focusing on construction equipment, while outlook for Singapore and Hong Kong remains robust.

data.title: Alchemy Pay Completed Type 1 and Type 4 License Upgrade in Hong Kong
data.description:
Alchemy Pay announced that, together with SFC-licensed HTF Securities Limited, it completed the upgrade of its SFC Type 1 license to include virtual asset trading services. The company also said the Type 1 and Type 4 license upgrades are now complete, while the Type 9 license upgrade remains in progress.

Refer to the official tweet by ACH:

Alchemy Pay|$ACH: Fiat-Crypto Payment Gateway
@AlchemyPay

🇭🇰 #AlchemyPay, together with SFC-licensed HTF Securities Limited, is pleased to announce the successful uplift of SFC Type 1 license to include virtual asset trading services.

With this upgrade secured, we have completed the Type 1 & Type 4 license upgrade, while the Type 9â€¦ pic.twitter.com/j7WoU17M0n

Mar 26, 2026

ACH Info

Alchemy Pay is a payment gateway that seamlessly connects crypto and global fiat currencies for businesses, developers, and users. Alchemy Pay’s on & off ramp solution and NFT checkout is integrated via plugin or API. It provides platforms and token issuers with an easy onboarding from fiat currency to crypto. Alchemy Pay supports payments from 173 countries with Visa, Mastercard, Apple Pay, Google Pay, regional mobile wallets, and domestic bank transfers, with a focus on emerging markets.

Its real-world crypto payment solutions facilitate payments for Binance Pay, Solana Pay and Crypto.com Pay, and e-commerce merchants in 70+ countries.

data.title: Petrobras to boost April fuel supply via contracts, ditching auctions
data.description:
By Marta Nogueira

Brazil's Petrobras has shifted its fuel supply strategy and will offer additional fuel volumes to distributors for April deliveries, the state-run oil company told Reuters on Thursday.

Petrobras said it will supply an additional 70 million liters of S10 diesel and 95 million liters of gasoline next month, with the extra volumes delivered through existing contracts rather than via diesel and gasoline auctions.

The change means fuel will be sold at lower prices than if it were being auctioned off, sources told Reuters, helping keep domestic prices in check amid a recent surge triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.

Petrobras had been negotiating additional fuel volumes via auctions, but abruptly canceled them after premiums ranging from 1.80 to 2.00 reais ($0.3445-0.3828) per liter of diesel over refinery prices emerged, according to four sources familiar with the matter.

The halt to the auctions, made without explanation early last week, intensified supply concerns for April, prompting oil regulator ANP to order the oil firm to release the volumes.

In a bid to soften the impact of higher global oil prices on local diesel costs, Brazil's government has also scrapped federal taxes on the fuel, while ANP campaigned to fight price gouging at gas stations.

Rising fuel prices are a major concern for President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva as he seeks reelection this year.

($1 = 5.2253 reais)

data.title: Stock indexes, bond prices fall as Iran crisis pushes oil above $105
data.description:
Key points:
  • US stocks down, with Nasdaq leading losses
  • Investors wary as Iran reviews US proposal
  • Investors worry about energy price shocks

By Caroline Valetkevitch and Marc Jones

Major stock indexes eased on Thursday as Brent oil futures rose above $105 a barrel, with Iran's denial of any talks with the U.S. dimming hopes of a quick resolution to the nearly one-month-long Middle East war.

Global debt markets also sold off, pushing yields higher, while safe-haven buying boosted the U.S. dollar.

Prospects of a prolonged war in the Middle East fanned worries about energy supply disruptions. Oil and European natural gas rose, with Brent futures up $4.77 at $106.99 a barrel and U.S. crude futures up at $93.64.

U.S. President ​Donald Trump warned Iran on Thursday to "get serious" about a deal to end nearly four weeks of fighting.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi had earlier said Tehran was reviewing the U.S. proposal but that there were no talks on winding down the war. Iran on Thursday launched multiple waves of missiles at Israel.

The war, triggered by U.S.–Israeli strikes on Iran in late February, has rattled global markets and effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit for a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas flows.

Stocks fell "as oil prices resumed their upward climb", said Peter Cardillo, chief market economist at Spartan Capital Securities in New York.

"Unfortunately, we're in a market that's being driven by oil prices. The rhetoric back and forth is continuing, and until talks begin, the market is going to be subject to the price of oil," he said.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 75.50 points, or 0.19%, to 46,342.69, the S&P 500 fell 43.59 points, or 0.68%, to 6,547.14 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 216.95 points, or 1.02%, to 21,705.16.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI dropped 6.75 points, or 0.68%, to 988.71. The pan-European STOXX 600 index fell 0.64%.

Japan's Nikkei ended down 0.3%, while worries over rising energy costs hammered South Korea's KOSPI , which slumped 3.2%. Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 1.9% and China's blue chips dropped 1.3%.

The Philippines held an unscheduled central bank meeting due to the turmoil, while Germany's central bank head said an ECB rate hike next month was "an option".

Fears of a 2022-style inflation shock have seen traders fully price out any chance of a Federal Reserve rate cut this year, further supporting the dollar.

Germany's two-year bond yield (DE2YT=RR), sensitive to European Central Bank rate expectations, rose after falling on Wednesday. Bond yields move inversely to prices.

Worries about persistent inflation also drove U.S. Treasury yields higher. The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was last up 4.2 basis points at 4.37%. The two-year note's yield (US2YT=RR) was last up 5.4 bps at 3.934%.

Earlier, the yield on Japan's two-year government bond (JP2YT=RR) hit its highest level in 30 years at 1.33%, as traders cemented bets on another Bank of Japan rate hike as early as next month.

In currencies, the U.S. dollar rose against most major currencies, reviving its safe-haven appeal.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, rose 0.1% to 99.75, with the euro down 0.13% at $1.1544.

Against the Japanese yen , the dollar strengthened 0.04% to 159.53.

Gold retreated as the dollar rose. Spot gold was down 0.89% at $4,465.06 an ounce.

data.title: Hormuz closure is a physical, not financial, issue
data.description:
By Robert Cyran

The White House seems to consider insurance a key hurdle for transit through the war-torn waterway. Yet brokers say they’re offering coverage. At 6% of a ship’s value, the price has gone up a lot. But the bigger risk is that replacing an out-of-commission vessel takes years.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Robert Cyran on Bluesky.

CONTEXT NEWS

Iranian officials said on March 24 that "non-hostile" ships may transit the Strait of Hormuz in coordination with Iranian authorities. On the same day, President Donald Trump said the United States was talking to the "right people in Iran and they want to make a deal so badly". Since the United States and Israel launched strikes on Iran in February, few ships have passed through the Strait, which handled shipments equivalent to a fifth of global oil and gas demand prior to the conflict.

data.title: TSX gains on industrials, consumer strength amid Mideast war overhang
data.description:
By Rashika Singh and Ragini Mathur

Industrials and consumer stocks lifted Canada's benchmark index on Thursday, even as investors remained cautious amid mixed signals from the U.S. and Iran over the Middle East conflict.

At 10:30 a.m. ET, the Toronto Stock Exchange's S&P/TSX composite index was up 0.3% at 32,437.37.

The index was on track for a fourth straight session of gains with eight of the 11 major sectors trading in the green despite lingering uncertainty over the war.

Heavyweight industrials (.GSPTTIN) rose 0.5%, while consumer staples (.GSPTTCS) and consumer discretionary (.GSPTTCD) were up 0.7% and 0.5%, respectively.

U.S. President ​Donald Trump warned Tehran on Thursday to "get serious" about a deal to end nearly four weeks of fighting, after the Iranian foreign minister said his country was reviewing Washington's proposal but that there were no talks on winding down the war.

Oil rose more than 4% as the contradictory signals kept global markets on edge, with prospects for restoring flows through the key Strait of Hormuz still unclear.

The rise in crude pushed Canadian energy shares up 0.6%.

The materials sector   was flat after opening lower as gold retreated against a firmer U.S. dollar and on expectations of elevated interest rates on supply disruptions.

"It's a headline-driven market and the conviction is pretty weak," said Michael Dehal, a senior portfolio manager at Dehal Investment Partners at Raymond James.

"But amidst all this volatility, there are green shoots where we are seeing opportunities and I think gold for a long-term looks [like a] pretty good opportunity considering the fundamentals are still intact," he said.

Meanwhile, NATO's European allies and Canada increased defence spending by 20% in 2025 compared to the previous year, alliance chief Mark Rutte said in his annual report. Canada also plans to pitch a defence bank to the G7 to support small- and mid-sized defense suppliers that struggle to secure financing, Foreign Minister Anita Anand told Reuters.

Cogeco Communications fell 6.5% after TD Cowen downgraded it to "hold" from "buy".
